findings.description: search() fetches the ENTIRE non-deleted users table (or whole department) into memory via fetchall(), then filters/sorts/paginates in Python instead of pushing WHERE/ORDER BY/LIMIT/OFFSET to SQLite, making it O(N) memory and O(N log N) CPU per request just to return a fixed-size page.

The full result set is sorted in Python on every request (users.sort key=display_name.lower()) instead of via an indexed ORDER BY in SQL, so sort cost scales with total matching rows rather than the page size and cannot use any DB index.

Pagination is done by slicing a fully-materialized in-memory list (users[offset:offset+page_size]) after fetching all rows, wasting the work of building/filtering/sorting every row only to discard all but page_size of them.

name_pattern regex is recompiled on every call (no cache) and matcher.search is run row-by-row in Python across the entire fetched set; this cannot use a DB index and, since name_pattern is an untrusted regex, a catastrophic-backtracking pattern amplifies one request into a full CPU pin (DoS angle deferred to critic-security).

Every public method opens and tears down a brand-new sqlite3 connection via _connect() (lines 40/70/82/93) with no reuse or pooling, paying file-open + cold-page-cache + no-prepared-statement-reuse overhead on each request.

offset = page * page_size for a documented 1-based page is an off-by-one (page 1 skips the first window) and is a symptom of the hand-rolled in-memory pagination; proper SQL LIMIT ? OFFSET (page-1)*page_size would fix correctness and eliminate the fetch-everything-to-slice pattern (correctness detail deferred to critic-logic).

departments() runs SELECT DISTINCT department ... WHERE deleted = 0 ORDER BY department which will full-scan users absent a covering index on (deleted, department); flagged at low confidence since no schema/DDL is in scope to confirm indexing.
